verses.commentary: Jesus dined at a ruler of the Pharisees' house on a Sabbath. They were watching him closely (paratereo, παρατηρέω — observed for hostile purposes). Hospitality with surveillance.
A man with dropsy (hydropikos, ὑδρωπικός — fluid retention) was in his presence. Possibly a setup — or providential opportunity.
Jesus to lawyers and Pharisees: 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?' Direct question.
They remained silent. He took him, healed him, sent him away. Silence as answer; action follows.
'Which of you, having a son or an ox that has fallen into a well on a Sabbath, will not immediately pull him out?' Pragmatic point. Emergency overrides Sabbath restriction.
They could not reply. Silence again. The Sabbath argument is exhausted.
Noticing how guests chose places of honor — he told them a parable. Social hierarchy at table is the lens.
When invited to a wedding feast — do not sit in a place of honor. Someone more distinguished may have been invited.
Then the host will say, 'Give your place to this person' — and with shame you proceed to the lowest place.
When invited — go and sit in the lowest place. Host says, 'Friend, move up higher.' Then you will be honored before all. Honor is given, not seized.
'Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; he who humbles himself will be exalted.' Wisdom proverb pattern, kingdom-application.

To the host: 'When you give a dinner or banquet, do not invite friends, brothers, relatives, or rich neighbors — lest they invite you in return and you be repaid.' Reciprocal giving cycles back; gain is zero.
When you give a feast, invite the poor, crippled, lame, blind. Four categories of those who cannot reciprocate. Hospitality without reciprocation.
Blessed, because they cannot repay — you will be repaid at the resurrection of the just. Eschatological reward replaces social reciprocity.
One at the table: 'Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God.' Pious sentiment with assumed-inclusion.
Parable: a man gave a great banquet, invited many.
At the hour of the banquet — sent his servant to say: 'Come, everything is now ready.' Standard ancient pattern: pre-invitation and day-of summons.
All alike began to make excuses. First: 'I have bought a field, must go see it.' Lame excuse — no one buys land without seeing it first.
Another: 'I have bought five yoke of oxen and must examine them.' Same pattern — no one buys oxen without examining first.
Another: 'I have married a wife, therefore I cannot come.' Marriage cited as exemption (compare Deuteronomy 24:5 — new groom exempt from war). The excuses are increasingly trivial.
Servant reports. Master angry — 'Go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city, bring in the poor, crippled, blind, lame.' Same list as 14:13. The mercy-fulfilled banquet.
Servant: 'What you commanded has been done; there is still room.'
Master: 'Go out to the highways and hedges and compel (anangkason, ἀνάγκασον) people to come in, that my house may be filled.' Gentile inclusion implied — the periphery.
'None of those originally invited will taste my banquet.' The first-invited forfeit by refusing.
Great crowds traveling with him. Turning to them — discipleship discourse begins.

'If anyone comes to me and does not hate (miseo, μισεῖ — Semitic 'love less by comparison') his father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters — yes, even his own life — he cannot be my disciple.' Comparative loyalty. Christ outranks all natural ties.
Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple. Cross-bearing — the daily condemned-prisoner identity.
Which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost?
Lest, having laid a foundation and not able to finish — all who see begin to mock.
'This man began to build and was not able to finish.' Public shame for incomplete projects.
Or what king, going to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to oppose twenty thousand?
If not, while the other is far off, sends a delegation and asks terms of peace. Realistic assessment matters.
So therefore — anyone who does not renounce (apotassomai, ἀποτάσσομαι) all that he has cannot be my disciple. Discipleship has a price; calculate it.
Salt is good — but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored?
Not fit for soil or for the manure pile — thrown away. 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear.' Useless disciple is unsalvageable; the warning is severe.
